---
title: "AI"
---

The AI application page tracks phase channels, coil attention, cyclic memory, looped/recursive transformer schedules, and adapter/block indexing. Local compute language is MLX/Mac-first.

The current proved layer is finite indexing only. `AIA-B0001` adds an exploratory deterministic phase-channel benchmark fixture for a known-period synthetic task. `AIA-B0002` adds a tiny learned-baseline control: phase lookup wins on a periodic task, while an ordinary scalar threshold wins on a nonperiodic control. `AIA-B0003` adds CPU/optional-MLX backend parity scoring across the current deterministic AI fixtures; if MLX is unavailable, it reports that directly. `AIA-B0004` adds learned-feature baseline controls: cyclic phase features are compared with dense-scalar, learned-position, and wrong-period baselines, plus a nonperiodic scalar-control task. `AIA-B0005` adds harmonic/Fourier feature controls: cyclic phase lookup and correct sine/cosine lookup solve the constructed periodic task, while wrong-frequency, scalar, and learned-position baselines remain controls. `AIM-B0001` adds the same discipline to cyclic memory: a positive slot-lookup fixture, constant/scalar-threshold baselines, collision diagnostics, and a nonperiodic control. `AIM-B0002` adds a coil-retrieval reachability fixture: a selected coil path reaches a constructed long-lag dependency, local and wrong-stride baselines miss it, full attention is the oracle upper bound, and local attention wins the near-lag control. `AIRA-B0001` adds the adapter-block version: a positive block-index fixture, ordinary baselines, collision diagnostics, and a nonperiodic control. `AIRA-B0002` adds a MultiCoil/RoPE-style positional fixture: combined-period phase lookup beats single-period, constant, and scalar-threshold baselines on a constructed multi-period task, while the scalar threshold wins the nonperiodic control. `AIRA-B0003` adds a RoPE-style relative phase fixture: correct-period relative lookup recovers constructed query/key lag labels, while wrong-period, query-position, scalar-threshold, and nonperiodic controls remain separated. These are not model-quality claims.

::: {.learning-unit}

## Case-Study Frame

The circular address spaces are phase channels, memory slots, and adapter blocks. The serious AI question is whether explicit cyclic structure helps when the task actually has periodic structure. The current proved layer only certifies finite indexing behavior.

:::

## AI Program Map

AI is now a dedicated Circle Calculus program, not a side note. The avenues to explore are:

- phase channels and periodic feature routing,
- cyclic memory slots, alias diagnostics, and retrieval fixtures,
- coil/sparse attention for long-context path selection,
- looped and recursive transformer schedules with loop phase, exit certificates, and overthinking guardrails,
- adapter blocks, CoilRA, and block-cyclic parameter sharing,
- RoPE, MultiCoil RoPE, winding-aware positions, and torus-valued positional views,
- recurrent, state-space, and convolutional sequence models with explicit period structure,
- harmonic, Fourier, circulant, and NTT-adjacent features,
- quaternion, spherical, Hopf/fiber, and rotation-aware representations, and
- proof-carrying model components whose indexing or schedule behavior can be checked in Lean.

Every avenue needs ordinary baselines, negative controls, and reproducible scripts before it becomes a claim about model quality or speed.

## Looped Recurrence Path

Looped and recursive transformers reuse model blocks across multiple computation passes. The Circle Calculus version should make each pass explicit: loop phase, recurrence budget, state provenance, exit decision, and overthinking boundary. That is a possible interface contribution, not a proved model improvement.

The planned benchmark lane is `P5-EDGE-010`. It should compare against ordinary dense transformer depth, Universal Transformer recurrence, fixed looped transformers, adaptive early-exit models, recurrent-memory transformers, sparse/MoE looped models, RWKV/Mamba-style recurrent or state-space models, and nonrecursive transformer baselines.

The first lesson goal is to make recurrence readable:

```text
shared block
  -> loop phase
  -> state update
  -> exit or continue
  -> score trace
  -> overthinking boundary
```

The proof boundary stays strict: Lean can certify finite schedule and indexing facts; Python or MLX benchmarks must carry model-quality, runtime, memory, and parameter claims.

## Learning Path

1. Start with phase channels: finite indices, closure, and known-period examples.
2. Compare learned-feature baselines: cyclic features, dense scalar thresholds, learned absolute positions, and wrong-period controls.
3. Compare harmonic/Fourier features: correct sine/cosine phase features, wrong-frequency controls, and scalar controls.
4. Check backend parity: CPU references first, optional MLX only when available.
5. Add cyclic memory: slots, alias diagnostics, and nonperiodic controls.
6. Study coil retrieval: selected long-lag paths, local baselines, wrong-stride baselines, and full-attention oracle behavior.
7. Add looped recurrence: loop phase, exit certificates, score traces, over-loop controls, and recursive transformer baselines.
8. Move to adapters and MultiCoil positions: block sharing, RoPE-style phase structure, and mismatch controls.
9. Compare RoPE-style relative phase: query/key lag labels, wrong-period controls, query-only controls, and scalar controls.
10. Only then compare learned model baselines and MLX prototypes, with quality, runtime, memory, parameter count, and interpretability reported separately.
11. Treat quaternion, spherical, Hopf/fiber, and `S^7` ideas as later geometry-aware AI lessons, not as shortcuts around the lower-dimensional evidence.

## What To Notice

- Phase-channel proofs are about finite indices, not model quality.
- `AIA-B0003` is backend parity scaffolding, not a speed, quality, or acceleration claim.
- `AIA-B0004` is a learned-feature baseline scaffold, not a neural-network result or real-workload benchmark.
- `AIA-B0005` is a harmonic/Fourier-feature scaffold, not a standard RoPE or language-model benchmark.
- Positive periodic fixtures must be paired with negative controls.
- `AIM-B0001` is a cyclic-memory benchmark harness, not a retrieval or attention theorem.
- `AIM-B0002` is a reachability harness over candidate index sets, not an attention-quality theorem.
- `COMMON-0052` through `COMMON-0054` are planned looped/recursive transformer vocabulary terms, not model-quality results.
- `AIRA-B0001` is an adapter-block benchmark harness, not a CoilRA, RoPE, parameter-efficiency, or quality theorem.
- `AIRA-B0002` is a MultiCoil/RoPE-style positional harness, not a standard-RoPE comparison or language-model theorem.
- `AIRA-B0003` is a RoPE-style relative phase harness, not an attention-quality, context-length, or language-model theorem.
- MLX/Mac-first experiments should be reported as benchmarks, not proofs.

::: {.checkpoint-box}

## Checkpoint

Why is the nonperiodic control in `AIA-B0002` important before making any AI-improvement claim?

:::

## Source Trail

<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIA-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIA-T0004"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIA-T0005"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0004"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIM-T0005"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0001"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0004"></div>
<div class="theorem-box" data-theorem-id="AIRA-T0005"></div>

<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0039"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0046"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0047"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0048"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0049"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0050"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0051"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0052"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0053"></div>
<div class="dictionary-box" data-dictionary-id="COMMON-0054"></div>

<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_01_CIRCLE_AI_ARCHITECTURES.md">AI architecture paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_01_CIRCLE_AI_ARCHITECTURES.md">Circle AI Architectures</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY.md">Memory paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_02_COIL_ATTENTION_AND_MEMORY.md">Coil Attention And Memory</a></p>
<p data-paper-ref="papers/applications/PAPER_AI_03_COILRA_AND_MULTICOIL_ROPE.md">Adapter paper: <a href="https://github.com/corbensorenson/circle-calculus/blob/main/papers/applications/PAPER_AI_03_COILRA_AND_MULTICOIL_ROPE.md">CoilRA And MultiCoil RoPE</a></p>

::: {.warning-box}
Python and optional MLX benchmark fixtures are executable references only. They do not replace Lean proofs and do not prove that circles improve neural networks.
:::

<script type="module" src="../../widgets/shared/widget_base.js"></script>
